title: Braiding Sweetgrass: Indigenous Wisdom, Scientific Knowledge, and the Teachings of Plants
excerpt: A botanist and member of the Citizen Potawatomi Nation braids together Indigenous wisdom, scientific knowledge, and personal story to reveal the gifts of the natural world.
keyIdeas: Indigenous knowledge and Western science are complementary ways of understanding the natural world
Plants are not resources but teachers, relatives, and gift-givers
Reciprocity - giving back to the land that sustains us - is the foundation of ecological ethics
Gratitude and attention are the first steps toward healing our relationship with nature
keyTakeaways: Sweetgrass grows stronger when harvested - a demonstration of reciprocity in action
The Three Sisters (corn, beans, squash) model a sustainable system of mutual support
Language shapes perception - the Potawatomi language's verb-based worldview sees the world as alive and active
Restoring ecological health requires restoring Indigenous relationships to land
whoShouldRead: Anyone feeling despair about the environmental crisis
Students of botany, ecology, and environmental studies
Indigenous and non-Indigenous readers seeking cultural reconciliation
Fans of poetic, reflective nature writing
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking a technical botanical textbook
Those uncomfortable with Indigenous spiritual perspectives in a science context
